critique.summary: The article documents the author's process of building a simple Unix shell called 'andsh' in C, covering features like command execution, environment variable expansion, piping, and basic line editing with history and tab completion. It explains key shell concepts and system calls such as fork, execvp, pipe, and dup2, illustrating how these are used to implement shell functionality.
mainPoints: The author builds a toy Unix shell named 'andsh' to understand how shells work under the hood.
Basic shell features implemented include running commands, environment variable expansion, built-in commands like cd, and piping between processes.
The shell uses Unix system calls like fork, execvp, waitpid, pipe, and dup2 to manage processes and interprocess communication.
Line editing, command history, and tab completion are added using the readline library.
The shell is intentionally incomplete, lacking features like quoting, redirection, and advanced built-ins.
The author reflects on the complexity of shell implementation and low-level process management.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the implementation details of a simple Unix shell in C, correctly explaining the use of system calls such as fork, execvp, waitpid, pipe, and dup2, which are standard and well-documented in Unix programming. The explanation of shell concepts like built-in commands needing to run in the parent process (e.g., cd), environment variable expansion, and piping aligns with established shell behavior. The use of the readline library for line editing and history is also a common practice. Since the article is a first-person technical blog post about the author's own coding project, the claims about the shell's capabilities and limitations are consistent with typical toy shell implementations and do not contradict known facts.
considerations: The shell lacks support for quoting and redirection, which are essential features in practical shells.
Error handling and security considerations are minimal and would need enhancement for production use.
Performance implications of features like tab completion scanning directories are noted but not deeply analyzed.
The shell does not support advanced scripting features or job control, which are common in full-featured shells.
The article does not provide external sources or comparisons to other shell implementations.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and learning experiences involved in building a Unix shell, with emphasis on string parsing, pipes, job control, and the complexity hidden beneath the shell's simplicity. Several commenters share personal experiences and resources, highlighting both frustrations and rewarding moments.

comments.author: wei03288
comments.text: The pipe section is the part that changes how you think about processes. Once you've manually done the dup2 dance — close write-end in parent, close read-end in child, wire them up — it stops being magic and starts being obvious why `grep | sort | uniq` works at all. The thing that surprised me building a similar toy was how late in the process job control has to come: you can get a working pipe chain surprisingly fast, and then job control (SIGTSTP, tcsetpgrp, the whole mess) costs 5x more than everything else combined.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate insight into the complexity of pipes and job control in shells, reflecting deep understanding and practical experience.

comments.author: rigorclaw
comments.text: The pipe implementation section is really clean. Working through fork/exec/dup2 by hand like this is one of those exercises that makes you appreciate how much composability Unix got right. Processes that know nothing about each other just work together because they read stdin and write stdout. I built something similar years ago and the moment pipes actually worked felt like unlocking a cheat code.
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully appreciates the composability of Unix processes and the learning milestone of implementing pipes, aligning well with known shell behavior.

comments.author: lvales
comments.text:
Building a shell is a great exercise, but honestly having to deal with string parsing is such a bother that it robs like 2/3 of the joy along the way. I once built a very simple one in Go [0] as a learning exercise and I stopped once I started getting frustrated with all the corner cases.

[0] https://github.com/lourencovales/codecrafters/blob/master/sh...
comments.analysis: The comment accurately reflects common frustrations with string parsing in shell building, supported by personal experience and a linked example.

comments.author: hexer303
comments.text: Unix shells are conceptually simple but hide a surprising amount of complexity under the hood that we take for granted. I recently had build my own PTY controller. There were so many edge-cases to deal with. It took weeks of stress testing and writing many tests to get it right.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly emphasizes the hidden complexity in shell implementation, supported by the author's experience with PTY controllers.

comments.author: zokier
comments.text: Bit of pedantry but I don't think traditional unix shell (like this) follows repl model; the shell is not usually doing printing of the result of evaluation. Instead the printing happens more as a side effect of the commands.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a precise technical correction about the shell's evaluation and printing model, adding nuance to the discussion.

comments.author: ratzkewatzke
comments.text: There's a very good exercise on Codecrafters (https://app.codecrafters.io/courses/shell/overview) to walk you through writing your own shell. I found it enlightening, as well as a good way to learn a new language.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a helpful resource for learning shell building, which is relevant and likely accurate.

comments.author: emersion
comments.text: Some time ago I've written an article about a particular aspect of shells, job control: https://emersion.fr/blog/2019/job-control/
comments.analysis: The comment shares a relevant article link on job control, contributing useful information to the topic.

comments.author: mzs
comments.text:
Had an assignment to build a shell in a week, how hard could it be?

  controlling terminal
  session leader
  job control
The parser was easy in comparison.
comments.analysis: The comment highlights the relative difficulty of job control and terminal management compared to parsing, which aligns with common developer experiences.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: Interesting. I wanted to do toast | bash to let the AI drive the computer but the bash shell really got in the way. Too much complexity. The things that annoy humans, $ expansion, special characters, etc don't work for AI either. Ended up writing a custom shell for AI (and humans). When a tool gets in the way, sometimes it just time to change the tool.
comments.analysis: This comment offers an interesting perspective on shell complexity interfering with AI use, which is plausible though more subjective.

comments.author: lioeters
comments.text: Link was previously posted by author: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47398749 There are other good quality articles on their site, and maybe deserves the imaginary points.
comments.analysis: This comment notes prior posting of a link and praises the quality of the article, adding minor value to the discussion.

comments.author: austy69
comments.text: Fun read. Wonder if you are able to edit text in the shell, or if you need to implement a gap buffer to allow it?
comments.analysis: This comment poses a relevant question about shell text editing capabilities, though it does not provide factual information.
